comments:
This paper presents a useful and well-motivated method for large-scale discovery of metaphorical framing using hierarchical graph factorization clustering (HGFC) applied to corpora in English, Spanish and Russian, and links corpus-derived differences to human behaviour via a cross-linguistic experiment. Strengths: the unsupervised approach is portable across languages, the idea of using verb co-occurrence clustering of nouns to reveal conceptual mappings is sensible and grounded in prior work, and the behavioural validation is an important step toward demonstrating psychological relevance. The paper is generally well written and presents technical details of HGFC clearly.

Concerns and suggestions: (1) Evaluation and baselines are limited. The only computational baseline is an agglomerative clustering variant; comparisons to additional baselines (e.g. distributional similarity with modern embeddings, other soft-clustering or topic models, or existing supervised metaphor detectors) would strengthen claims of superiority. (2) Quantitative reporting could be more complete: the paper reports precision/recall for a subset of source domains and agreement statistics, but lacks confidence intervals, significance testing for the computational comparisons, and more exhaustive error analysis. (3) Some methodological choices are under-justified: why 2000 nouns, why initial m1=800, and how sensitive are results to these settings? (4) Cross-linguistic claims may be confounded by corpus differences and parsing quality (authors note Gigaword vs. web RU-WaC); stronger controls (matched genres or additional corpora) or more discussion of these confounds are needed. (5) The behavioural experiment is a valuable addition, but reporting is incomplete (final sample sizes after exclusions, full model coefficients, effect sizes, and robustness checks). More detail on participant recruitment and language proficiency controls would help assess the strength of the psycholinguistic claim.

Overall, the paper makes a meaningful contribution by combining unsupervised metaphor discovery with behavioural validation; with stronger comparative evaluation, robustness analyses, and fuller reporting of experimental details, it would be an even stronger contribution. I recommend acceptance and an oral presentation so the authors can discuss methodological specifics and behavioural implications with the community.
